file_name: Array BioPharma Inc. - LICENSE, DEVELOPMENT AND COMMERCIALIZATION AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: 1.1 "Affiliate" of a Party means any person, corporation or other entity that, directly or indirectly, controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with such Party, as the case may be. As used in this Section 1.1, the word "control" (including, with correlative meaning, the terms "controlled by" or "under the common control with") shall mean the actual power, either directly or indirectly through one or more intermediaries, to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of such entity, whether by the ownership of more than fifty percent (50%) of the voting share capital in such person, corporation, or other entity, or by contract or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 "Affiliate" of a Party means any person, corporation or other entity that, directly or indirectly, controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with such Party, as the case may be. As used in this Section 1.1, the word "control" (including, with correlative meaning, the terms "controlled by" or "under the common control with") shall mean the potential power, either directly or indirectly through one or more intermediaries, to influence the direction of the management and policies of such entity, whether by informal arrangements or otherwise. Ownership percentages are irrelevant in this determination.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Affiliate" defines "control" as 'actual power' based on ownership or contract. The modified definition changes "actual power" to "potential power" and removes the objective criteria of ownership, thus conflicting with how 'control' is generally understood and legally defined in corporate law. The phrase 'potential power' has no grounding in accepted business practices. It also removes the concept of ownership of 50%, which is misaligned with federal laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Definition of Control
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text deviates from the SEC's definition of 'control' under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, which relies on the power to direct management and policies, often linked to ownership. Removing the criteria of ownership and introducing 'potential power' misaligns the contract with established regulatory definitions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 230.405 - Definitions of terms. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 230.405 - Definitions of terms. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 230.405 Definitions of terms. Unless the context otherwise requires, all terms used in §§ 230.400 to 230.494, inclusive, or in the forms for registration have the same meanings as in the Act and in the general rules and regulations . In addition, the following definitions apply, unless the context otherwise requires: Affiliate. An affiliate of, or person affiliated with, a specified person, is a person that directly, or indirectly through one or more intermediaries, controls or is controlled by, or is under common control with, the person specified. Amount. The term amount, when used in regard to securities, means the principal amount if relating to evidences of indebtedness, the number of shares if relating to shares , and the number of units if relating to any other kind of security . Associate. The term associate, when used to indicate a relationship with any person, means (1) a corporation or organization (other than the registrant or a majority-owned subsidiary of the registrant) of which such person is an officer or partner or is, directly or indirectly, the beneficial owner of 10 percent or more of any class of equity securities, (2) any trust or other estate in which such person has a substantial beneficial interest or as to which such person serves as trustee or in a similar capacity, and (3) any relative or spouse of such person, or any relative of such spouse, who has the same home as such person or who is a director or officer of the registrant or any of its parents or subsidiaries. Automatic shelf registration statement. The term automatic shelf registration statement means a registration statement filed on Form S-3, Form F-3, or Form N-2 ( § 239.13 , § 239.33 , or §§ 239.14 and 274.11a-1 of this chapter) by

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts established legal definitions of 'control' as it relates to affiliates, particularly regarding the power to direct management and policies, which is often tied to ownership. The original text defines 'control' with objective criteria, including ownership, while the changed text introduces 'potential power' and disregards ownership percentages. Scraped Law Snippet 1, derived from 17 CFR § 230.405, defines 'Affiliate' as a person who 'directly, or indirectly through one or more intermediaries, controls or is controlled by, or is under common control with, the person specified.' This definition implicitly supports the importance of actual control, often associated with ownership, rather than potential influence. By removing ownership as a relevant factor and focusing on 'potential power,' the changed text deviates from the SEC's understanding of control and creates a legally questionable definition of 'Affiliate,' creating a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1.25 "Good Manufacturing Practices or "GMP" means all laws and guidelines applicable to the Manufacture of Binimetinib, Encorafenib or Product, including (i) the FD&C Act (21 U.S.C. 321 et seq.); (ii) relevant United States regulations in Title 21 of the United States Code of Federal Regulations (including Parts 11, 210, and 211); (iii) European Community Directives 2001/83/EC and 2003/94/EC; (iv) the EU Guidelines to Good Manufacturing Practice Medicinal Products for Human and Veterinary Use, as set out in Volume 4 of the European Commission's Rules governing medicinal products in the EU; (v) those standards required by the Japanese Ministry of Health Labour and Welfare (MHLW); (vi) International Conference on Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Registration of Pharmaceuticals for Human Use ("ICH"), Q7 Good Manufacturing Practice Guidance for Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients; (vii) similar standards and Laws to those in (i) through (vi), as are in effect at the time of Manufacture; and (viii) all additional Regulatory Authority documents or regulations that replace, amend, modify, supplant or complement any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.25 "Good Manufacturing Practices or "GMP" means general guidelines and industry suggestions applicable to the Manufacture of Binimetinib, Encorafenib or Product, including suggestions from (i) the FD&C Act (21 U.S.C. 321 et seq.); (ii) United States regulations in Title 21 of the United States Code of Federal Regulations (including Parts 11, 210, and 211); (iii) European Community Directives 2001/83/EC and 2003/94/EC; (iv) the EU Guidelines to Good Manufacturing Practice Medicinal Products for Human and Veterinary Use, as set out in Volume 4 of the European Commission's Rules governing medicinal products in the EU; (v) standards suggested by the Japanese Ministry of Health Labour and Welfare (MHLW); (vi) International Conference on Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Registration of Pharmaceuticals for Human Use ("ICH"), Q7 Good Manufacturing Practice Guidance for Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients; (vii) similar suggestions to those in (i) through (vi), as are in effect at the time of Manufacture; and (viii) additional documents or guidelines that may replace, amend, modify, supplant or complement any of the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of GMP references specific laws, guidelines, and regulations that are mandatory for pharmaceutical manufacturing. The modified definition weakens the requirement by replacing "laws and guidelines" with "general guidelines and industry suggestions" and replacing "standards required" with "standards suggested", which misrepresents the legally binding nature of GMP requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the FDA's legally enforceable GMP regulations outlined in 21 CFR Parts 210 and 211. GMPs are not merely suggestions but are legally mandated requirements for ensuring drug safety and efficacy during manufacturing. The modification creates a legal uncertainty about the applicable manufacturing standards.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original intent by weakening the mandatory nature of Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP). The original text defines GMP as "all laws and guidelines applicable," while the changed text refers to GMP as "general guidelines and industry suggestions." This undermines the legal enforceability of GMP standards, as referenced in 21 CFR Part 210, which outlines "Current Good Manufacturing Practice in Manufacturing, Processing, Packing, or Holding of Drugs." By changing mandatory requirements into mere suggestions, the modified text creates a legal contradiction by misaligning the terminology used with its statutory meaning, making the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 1.28 "IND" means an investigational new drug application (including any amendments thereto) filed with the PMDA before the commencement of Clinical Studies for a Product in Japan, or any comparable filing with any Regulatory Authority in any other jurisdiction within or outside the Ono Territory (including any Investigational New Drug Application filed with a Regulatory Authority in the United States pursuant to 21 C.F.R. §321).
perturbation.changed_text: 1.28 "IND" means a notice to a Regulatory Authority that a company intends to conduct some tests on animals or humans for a new Product in Japan, or any comparable notification with any Regulatory Authority in any other jurisdiction within or outside the Ono Territory (including a letter to a Regulatory Authority in the United States pursuant to recommendations outlined in 21 C.F.R. §321).
perturbation.explanation: The original text correctly defines IND as an investigational new drug application. The modified text redefines it as simply "a notice" and replaces the legal term of "application" with that of a "letter". It also inaccurately describes clinical trials as "tests on animals or humans".  The original reference to 21 C.F.R. §321 is also replaced by 'recommendations outlined in', changing the mandatory character of the law with a voluntary one.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the formal IND requirements defined in 21 U.S. Code § 355, which mandate a comprehensive application with specific data and information. This application needs to be approved before interstate shipment and clinical investigation of a new drug can occur. The modification attempts to describe the IND process as an informal notice.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 21 U.S. Code § 355, as it redefines 'IND' from a formal 'investigational new drug application' to an informal 'notice'. This contradicts the law's requirement for an 'approval of an application filed pursuant to subsection (b) or (j)' before introducing a new drug into interstate commerce, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'No person shall introduce or deliver for introduction into interstate commerce any new drug, unless an approval of an application filed pursuant to subsection (b) or (j) is effective with respect to such drug.'
